title: Security
lede: iMash protects your business with per-person logins, granular permissions, single-session control, tamper-proof audit trails, and bank-grade data isolation. Here's your practical checklist to lock it all down in under ten minutes.

steps.title: Start at Team & Access — one login per person
steps.content:
Everything about account security starts with one golden rule: every person gets their own login. Shared logins make it impossible to know who changed what, and you can't cut off one person without locking out everyone.

  1. In the left sidebar, click Team & Access. The User Management page opens on the Users tab.
  2. Review the list: every row is one person, with their Role, Groups, View Profile, and Status (Active or Disabled).
  3. If two people currently share one login, split them now — click Add User to give the second person their own account (next step).
💡 The tabs across the top — Users, Roles, Permissions, Groups, and View Profiles — are your complete access-control toolkit. This guide focuses on the security essentials; the Team Management guide covers each tab in depth.

images.alt: User Management page showing the Users tab with per-person accounts, roles, groups, and status
images.caption: Team & Access → User Management — every person gets their own account with its own role and status

images.alt: User Management page showing the Users tab with per-person accounts, roles, groups, and status
images.caption: Team & Access → User Management — every person gets their own account with its own role and status

steps.title: Create accounts with strong, unique passwords
steps.content:
1. On the Users tab, click Add User.
  2. Fill in the fields in the Create User modal:
    • Email — the person's real work email (used for sign-in and password recovery).
    • Password — set a long, unique starting password. Never reuse a password from another service.
    • First Name / Last Name — shown across the app and in the audit trail, so activity is always attributable.
    • Role — pick the least-powerful role that covers their job (more on this in the next step).
  3. Click Create User and have the person change the starting password on first sign-in.
  4. If anyone ever forgets their password, they can use Forgot password on the sign-in screen to receive a secure reset link by email — you never need to share passwords over chat or SMS.
⚠️ A password manager beats memory every time. Encourage your team to generate 16+ character random passwords — the single cheapest security upgrade you can make.

images.alt: Create User modal with Email, Password, First Name, Last Name and Role fields
images.caption: The Create User modal — each account gets its own email, password, and least-privilege role

images.alt: Create User modal with Email, Password, First Name, Last Name and Role fields
images.caption: The Create User modal — each account gets its own email, password, and least-privilege role

steps.title: Grant the minimum access each person needs
steps.content:
The strongest account is the one that can't touch what it doesn't need. iMash layers three controls on top of each user:

  1. Role — on the Roles tab, roles define read/create/edit/delete rights per data type. Assign the least-powerful role that covers the job.
  2. Per-user Permissions — on the Users tab, click the grid icon (Permissions) on a team member's row. Toggle whole sidebar sections or single pages on and off. Pay special attention to the Account & Tools section:
    • API Keys — can issue and manage API keys. Keep Off unless they manage integrations.
    • Credentials — can manage shared provider credentials. Keep Off for most members.
    • User Management — can create users and change permissions. Keep Off for everyone except admins — this toggle controls access itself.
    Under Display Options, Hide Billing Prices and Hide Billing Page let members work with usage data without seeing dollar amounts.
  3. Groups and View Profiles — use the Groups tab so members only see records belonging to their team, and View Profiles to apply one saved permission set to many users at once.
💡 Ask "what would go wrong if this login were stolen?" — then turn off everything that answer includes. You can always re-enable a toggle in seconds.

images.alt: Per-user Permissions modal with section toggles including API Keys, Credentials and User Management
images.caption: The per-user Permissions modal — note the Account & Tools section where API Keys, Credentials, and User Management can be switched off

images.alt: Per-user Permissions modal with section toggles including API Keys, Credentials and User Management
images.caption: The per-user Permissions modal — note the Account & Tools section where API Keys, Credentials, and User Management can be switched off

steps.title: One session holds control at a time
steps.content:
iMash enforces single-session control: only one browser window actively controls an account at any moment.

  • If the same login opens somewhere new, the older window is instantly covered by a full-screen lock that reads "This account is in use on another device".
  • The locked window offers two buttons: Take control (reclaim the account on this device — the other screen locks in turn) and Log out.
  • Nothing underneath the lock screen is readable or clickable, so an unattended screen never leaks live data.
⚠️ If you see the lock screen when you didn't sign in anywhere else, treat it as a red flag — someone may have your password. Take control, then change your password immediately and review the Audit Log (step 7) for activity you don't recognize.

steps.title: Disable access the moment it's no longer needed
steps.content:
When someone leaves the company or changes roles, cut access in one click — no deleting, no data loss.

  1. Open Team & Access → Users and find the person's row.
  2. In the Actions column, click the red Disable button (circle-X icon) and confirm with Disable. Their sign-in stops working instantly; their records and history stay intact.
  3. If they return, click Enable (check icon) on the same row to restore access.

The other row actions are security tools too:

  • Permissions (grid icon) — the toggle editor from step 3.
  • Edit (pencil) — update name or role.
  • Impersonate (person-switch icon) — the account owner can temporarily view the app exactly as that member sees it, perfect for verifying that permissions actually hide what you intended. It's a time-limited support view; the member's password is never exposed.

images.alt: Row action buttons on a team member: Permissions, Edit, Impersonate, role permissions, and the red Disable button
images.caption: Row actions on each team member — the red circle-X disables sign-in instantly

images.alt: Row action buttons on a team member: Permissions, Edit, Impersonate, role permissions, and the red Disable button
images.caption: Row actions on each team member — the red circle-X disables sign-in instantly

steps.title: Practice API key hygiene
steps.content:
API keys are passwords for machines — treat them with the same care.

  1. In the top navigation, click Credentials, then open the API Keys page.
  2. Click Generate New API Key, give it a descriptive name (one key per integration — "Zapier", "Website form", "Reporting script"), and generate it.
  3. Copy the key immediately. The full secret is shown only once at creation; afterwards the list shows just the Key Prefix, so a leaked screenshot of this page can't compromise you.
  4. Audit the list regularly:
    • Last Used — a key that says Never or hasn't been used in months is pure risk with zero benefit. Delete it.
    • Created At — rotate long-lived keys periodically: generate a replacement, switch your integration over, then delete the old one.
  5. If a key may have been exposed (committed to a repo, pasted in a chat), delete it with the red trash icon right away and issue a fresh one.
💡 Because each integration has its own key, revoking one never breaks the others — that's why "one key per integration" is the golden rule.

images.alt: API Keys page listing keys with prefix, created date, last used and delete actions
images.caption: The API Keys page — only the prefix is ever displayed again, and Last Used exposes stale keys worth deleting

images.alt: API Keys page listing keys with prefix, created date, last used and delete actions
images.caption: The API Keys page — only the prefix is ever displayed again, and Last Used exposes stale keys worth deleting

steps.title: Verify everything with the Audit Log
steps.content:
Security you can't verify isn't security. iMash keeps an append-only Audit Log — a change history that records who changed what, when, across your account and every sub-account.

  1. In the left sidebar, click Audit.
  2. Each entry shows When, Who (with a Main/Sub tag), What Happened in plain language, and an Importance level.
  3. Filter by Table, Category, Severity, Action, Sub-account, or a date range to zero in on anything suspicious.
  4. Toggle Impersonated to see actions performed while the owner was impersonating a member — impersonated activity is always labeled, never hidden.

Use it after any security event: an unexpected session lock, a departed employee, or an integration behaving oddly. For alert rules and notifications, see the dedicated Audit Log guide in What's Next.

images.alt: Audit Log page with When, Who, What Happened and Importance columns plus filters
images.caption: The Audit Log — every change across your account and sub-accounts, filterable and attributable

images.alt: Audit Log page with When, Who, What Happened and Importance columns plus filters
images.caption: The Audit Log — every change across your account and sub-accounts, filterable and attributable

steps.title: What iMash handles for you — isolation, encryption, and the Trust Center
steps.content:
Beyond the controls you configure, the platform protects you by design:

  • Data isolation — every account's data is separated with row-level security enforced by the database itself. Your leads, calls, and invoices are never visible to any other customer, and within your account the roles and toggles above decide exactly what each member can open.
  • Encryption — traffic is encrypted in transit (TLS) and data is encrypted at rest.
  • Payment safety — card payments are processed by Stripe (PCI DSS Level 1); full card numbers never touch iMash servers.

Want the receipts? Open the public Trust Center at /security on your dashboard domain. It documents compliance status (SOC 2, GDPR/DPA, HIPAA BAA), live third-party scans you can run yourself (SSL Labs, Mozilla Observatory), the platform architecture, the full sub-processor list, and the responsible-disclosure policy — share the link with any customer or auditor who asks how their data is protected.

images.alt: Trust Center compliance cards including SOC 2, GDPR DPA, HIPAA BAA, SSL Labs and responsible disclosure
images.caption: The public Trust Center at /security — compliance posture, live scans, and disclosure policy in one place

images.alt: Trust Center compliance cards including SOC 2, GDPR DPA, HIPAA BAA, SSL Labs and responsible disclosure
images.caption: The public Trust Center at /security — compliance posture, live scans, and disclosure policy in one place

support.content:
